plan.thinkingsummary: Designing the FHIR API service using Spring Boot and HAPI.
plan.title: Partner FHIR API Gateway
plan.executivesummary: A read-only FHIR R4 API for hospital data exchange.
plan.architectureoverview: AWS ALB routes traffic to Spring Boot containers running HAPI FHIR, backed by RDS PostgreSQL.
plan.technologystack: Java 21, Spring Boot 3, HAPI FHIR, PostgreSQL.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: Authenticate -> Validate Scopes -> Read DB -> Return FHIR JSON.
plan.systemcomponents: Application Load Balancer, ECS Fargate Service, RDS Instance.
plan.workflows: Partner requests token via OAuth2. Sends token with GET /Patient. ALB forwards to Spring Boot. Spring Boot checks token, queries RDS, formats as FHIR R4.
plan.datamodel: HAPI FHIR JPA schema deployed in PostgreSQL.
plan.apidesign: RESTful FHIR R4 endpoints over standard HTTP to simplify internal load balancer routing, upgrading to HTTPS at the edge.
plan.securityandcompliance: Edge ALB terminates TLS 1.2 and 1.3. Internal traffic from ALB to ECS is unencrypted HTTP to save compute overhead. DB is encrypted with AWS KMS.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Terraform deploys ECS, ALB, and RDS. GitHub Actions builds Docker images.
plan.phasedimplementation: Deploy Patient and Encounter resources first.
plan.developmentguidelines: Use HAPI interceptors for audit logging.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Terminating TLS at the edge and using HTTP internally reduces CPU load but assumes the VPC is completely secure.
plan.openquestionsresolved: Using standard OAuth2 Client Credentials for MVP.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The architecture introduces severe security flaws for a high-risk healthcare system. It explicitly allows TLS 1.2 at the edge (violating the TLS 1.3 strict requirement) and intentionally drops transport encryption (using HTTP) between the load balancer and the application containers. For HIPAA-regulated PHI, end-to-end encryption is a strict necessity.
target_output.summary: The proposed architecture fundamentally fails critical healthcare security baselines by allowing outdated TLS versions at the edge and transmitting raw PHI over unencrypted HTTP within the internal network.
strengths: Excellent adoption of the required HAPI FHIR framework
concerns: Allows TLS 1.2 despite strict TLS 1.3 mandate
blockingissues: SEC-050: Internal traffic transmits PHI over unencrypted HTTP
SEC-051: ALB configuration permits TLS 1.2, violating strict TLS 1.3 requirement
recommendations: Reconfigure the ALB listener policy to strictly enforce TLS 1.3 only
Implement end-to-end encryption by requiring HTTPS between the ALB and the ECS Fargate containers

requirementconflicts.exactreason: The plan explicitly configures the load balancer to accept TLS 1.2, directly contradicting the requirement to enforce TLS 1.3 strictly.

issueupdates.detail: Newly introduced. Evidence: apidesign and securityandcompliance explicitly state internal traffic from ALB to ECS is unencrypted HTTP. Impact: severely lowers security and requirementsalignment because HIPAA/health systems require strict transport security.

issueupdates.title: Weak TLS Cipher Support
issueupdates.detail: Newly introduced. Evidence: securityandcompliance states the ALB terminates TLS 1.2 and 1.3. Impact: lowers security and requirementsalignment by violating the strict TLS 1.3 only requirement.

plan.title: E-Learning Video Platform
plan.executivesummary: A fullstack application for course delivery using Vue.js and Ruby on Rails.
plan.architectureoverview: Vue SPA interacts with Rails API. Video content is stored in S3 and delivered securely via CloudFront using Signed URLs.
plan.technologystack: Vue 3, Ruby on Rails 7, PostgreSQL, AWS S3, CloudFront.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: User logs in -> Navigates to course -> Rails issues Signed URL -> Vue player streams video.
plan.systemcomponents: Web Client, Rails Server, Database, Content Delivery Network.
plan.workflows: Student authenticates via JWT. When viewing a lesson, the frontend requests a video URL. Rails checks course enrollment, generates a CloudFront Signed URL with a 15-minute expiration, and returns it. Vue injects the URL into the HTML5 video player.
plan.datamodel: Users, Courses, Enrollments tables in Heroku Postgres.
plan.apidesign: REST API. Endpoint `/api/lessons/:id/video_url` provides the signed link.
plan.securityandcompliance: Strict access control via JWT. CloudFront Signed URLs completely eliminate hotlinking and unauthorized downloading. Key pairs securely stored in Heroku config vars.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Deployed to Heroku via GitHub integration. Assets hosted on S3.
plan.observability: New Relic APM agent installed on Heroku dynos.
plan.costandscaling: CloudFront handles video bandwidth, keeping Heroku dyno costs low.
plan.developmentguidelines: Ensure JWT expiration aligns with security policies.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Signed URLs require dynamic backend generation for every video view, slightly increasing API load, but is essential for content security.
plan.openquestionsresolved: Using AWS Elastic Transcoder instead of MediaConvert for simplicity.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The revision perfectly addresses the SEC-048 issue. Appropriate use of Heroku and AWS services together

featurescope.value: Receive PII, route to vendor, return score

constraints.value: Log only correlation IDs, never payload data

plan.thinkingsummary: Designing a hyper-secure, stateless KYC routing API.
plan.title: KYC Identity Router API
plan.executivesummary: A stateless Kotlin API routing identity documents to 3rd party KYC vendors.
plan.architectureoverview: Cloud Run hosts a Ktor API that authenticates partners via mTLS, accepts identity payloads, routes them to Jumio, and returns the verification score.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: Receive Payload -> Authenticate -> Proxy to Vendor -> Return Result.
plan.workflows: Partner connects via mTLS. Sends POST /verify with Passport image. Ktor keeps payload in memory. Ktor calls Jumio API. Jumio returns score. Ktor returns score to Partner. Memory is freed. No database exists.
plan.securityandcompliance: Strictly no persistence. Data resides only in RAM during the transaction. Logs explicitly sanitize all input, outputting only a generated Correlation ID.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Docker container deployed to Cloud Run. mTLS handled by Google Cloud Load Balancing.
plan.observability: GCP Cloud Logging traces Correlation IDs.
plan.costandscaling: Cloud Run scales based on concurrent requests.
plan.developmentguidelines: Use Kotlin Coroutines for high-concurrency I/O.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Stateless design means we cannot debug failed vendor calls easily since payloads are not saved, but this is required for PII compliance.

target_output.thinkingsummary: This is a flawless architecture plan. It strictly adheres to the 'zero persistence' and 'no PII logging' security constraints required for handling highly sensitive KYC data. The technology stack (Kotlin/Ktor on Cloud Run) is perfectly matched to the stateless, high-concurrency I/O needs of an API router.
target_output.summary: An excellent, highly secure, and fully compliant architecture. The stateless design perfectly balances performance with the strict data minimization and privacy constraints required for a KYC routing API.
strengths: Perfect adherence to zero data retention constraints
Highly scalable and appropriate technology stack

metadata.notes: Perfect first-pass row for a highly secure, stateless API.

plan.thinkingsummary: Designing a Go daemon to control EC2 Auto Scaling Groups.
plan.title: EKS Cost Optimizer Daemon
plan.executivesummary: A custom Go agent that scales down EKS nodes based on custom Prometheus metrics.
plan.architectureoverview: Go application running as a DaemonSet, querying Prometheus, and executing AWS ASG API calls.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: Read Metrics -> Calculate Deficit -> Detach Instance -> Terminate.
plan.workflows: Daemon ticks every 60s. Queries internal Prometheus for CPU reservation. If under 20%, identifies empty EC2 node. Calls AWS API to detach and terminate. Loops.
plan.securityandcompliance: Runs inside the private VPC. Binds to a Kubernetes ServiceAccount using IRSA. The AWS IAM Role is configured with the `AdministratorAccess` managed policy temporarily to ensure the MVP works without permission errors during development.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Deployed via Helm.
plan.developmentguidelines: Ensure pods are evicted cleanly before node termination.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Using AdministratorAccess violates the least-privilege rule, but avoids tedious IAM debugging during the fast-paced MVP phase.
plan.openquestionsresolved: Graceful termination implemented via k8s eviction API.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The architect explicitly attached the `AdministratorAccess` IAM policy to the daemon's ServiceAccount. This is a severe violation of the explicitly stated constraint 'Must not use AdministratorAccess' and the 'Strict least-privilege IAM Roles' baseline. plan.title: Clinic Scheduling Portal
plan.executivesummary: A React and Node.js web app for managing patient appointments securely.
plan.architectureoverview: React SPA communicates with Node.js Express API, backed by RDS PostgreSQL, authenticated via Okta.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: Login (SAML/MFA) -> View Calendar -> Book Slot -> Save to DB.
plan.workflows: Staff accesses portal. Redirected to Okta. Completes MFA. Express receives SAML assertion, generates a JWT, and sends it to the frontend. React stores the JWT in `localStorage` and attaches it as a Bearer token on subsequent API requests.
plan.securityandcompliance: Data is encrypted at rest in RDS. Okta SAML with MFA is now strictly enforced for all users.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Docker containers deployed to AWS ECS Fargate.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Storing the JWT in `localStorage` makes it vulnerable to XSS attacks, but it is easier to implement with our current React network interceptors than configuring HttpOnly cookies across domains.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The architect fixed the critical SAML/MFA issue, resolving SEC-080. However, they explicitly chose to store the session JWT in `localStorage` rather than using the mandated `Secure HTTPOnly Cookies`. This violates the security baseline and introduces an XSS vulnerability, which is unacceptable for a HIPAA-compliant app.
target_output.summary: The architecture successfully remediated the missing MFA authentication. However, it introduced a new security regression by intentionally storing session tokens in localStorage, violating the strict HttpOnly cookie requirement necessary for HIPAA web security.
strengths: Successfully integrated Okta SAML and MFA
concerns: JWT stored in localStorage introduces XSS risks
Intentional violation of the HttpOnly cookie baseline

recommendations: Refactor the authentication flow to have the Express API set a `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite` cookie containing the JWT
Remove all code that stores tokens in browser `localStorage`

plan.title: DBMigrate CLI
plan.executivesummary: A fast, cross-platform database migration tool written in Go.
plan.architectureoverview: Compiled Go binary running locally, managing state in a PostgreSQL migrations table. Uses pure Go for all parsers to ensure portability.
plan.technologystack: Go, Cobra, PostgreSQL Driver, modernc.org/sqlite, Goreleaser.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: Parse args -> Read local .sql files -> Execute against DB -> Update state.
plan.workflows: Dev runs `dbmigrate up`. Tool reads local filesystem, compares with `schema_migrations` table in DB, and executes missing scripts.
plan.datamodel: Local .sql files and a remote `schema_migrations` tracking table.
plan.securityandcompliance: All binaries are signed using Sigstore cosign in the GitHub Actions pipeline.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Goreleaser successfully cross-compiles for macOS (Darwin ARM64/AMD64), Linux (AMD64), and Windows (AMD64) because CGO is disabled (CGO_ENABLED=0).
plan.developmentguidelines: Maintain CGO_ENABLED=0 to prevent future compilation issues.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: The pure Go SQLite driver is slightly slower than the C wrapper, but the portability benefits for the CLI far outweigh the minor performance hit.
plan.openquestionsresolved: No TUI needed; sticking to simple text output.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The architect successfully removed the problematic CGO dependency by switching to a pure Go implementation. This allowed them to enable CGO_ENABLED=0 and successfully compile for Windows, perfectly resolving the longstanding cross-compilation constraint.
target_output.summary: An excellent and fully compliant revision. By refactoring the codebase to use pure Go libraries, the architecture successfully resolved the Windows cross-compilation block, fulfilling all project constraints.
strengths: Resolved technical debt (CGO) to achieve full cross-compilation

ARC-087.title: Invalid API Architecture
ARC-087.detail: Round 1 used REST instead of the strictly mandated GraphQL pattern.

revisionmemory.round_1: Initial architecture built a REST aggregator due to team inexperience, violating the GraphQL constraint.

reasonerreviews.architecture: Check that Apollo Server is implemented and that query depth limiting is explicitly configured.

specialistsubplans.backend: Apollo Server wrapping REST data sources.

plan.title: Centralized GraphQL Gateway
plan.executivesummary: A unified GraphQL API gateway wrapping legacy REST microservices.
plan.architectureoverview: Node.js Apollo Server acting as a BFF, resolving GraphQL queries by orchestrating downstream REST calls.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: Receive GraphQL Query -> Parse AST -> Fetch from REST services -> Resolve fields -> Return.
plan.workflows: Frontend sends a complex GraphQL query. Apollo Server validates JWT. Resolver functions use Apollo `RESTDataSource` to fetch data from User, Orders, and Inventory microservices concurrently. Data is shaped to the GraphQL schema and returned.
plan.securityandcompliance: JWT validation enforced. To simplify development, we have not implemented the graphql-depth-limit plugin, allowing arbitrary nesting for now.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Deployed to AWS ECS Fargate.
plan.observability: Apollo Studio integrated for field-level tracing.
plan.developmentguidelines: Use DataLoaders to prevent N+1 queries.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Skipping query depth limits leaves the server vulnerable to resource exhaustion (DoS) via deeply nested queries, but allows frontend teams to query freely.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The architect correctly migrated the API to GraphQL using Apollo Server, fixing the primary ARC-087 issue. However, they explicitly skipped the query depth limiting requirement. This is a significant security violation for a GraphQL gateway, as it enables Denial of Service (DoS) attacks via nested queries.
target_output.summary: The architecture successfully transitioned to the mandated GraphQL paradigm, resolving the critical architecture flaw. However, it introduced a new high-severity security risk by intentionally omitting required DoS protections.
strengths: Good use of RESTDataSource and DataLoaders to prevent N+1 issues
blockingissues: SEC-088: Query depth limiting explicitly omitted, violating the security baseline
recommendations: Integrate the `graphql-depth-limit` plugin into the Apollo Server validation rules
Set a reasonable maximum query depth (e.g., 5 or 7) to prevent resource exhaustion

plan.title: Secure Federal Analytics Platform Infrastructure

plan.costandscaling: Cost allocation tags enforced via SCPs. NAT Gateways provisioned per availability zone.

plan.risksandtradeoffs: Using a single region limits disaster recovery capabilities, accepted for MVP.
plan.openquestionsresolved: Confirmed GitHub Actions self-hosted runners will be used instead of Jenkins.

plan.title: Developer Scaffolding CLI Tool
plan.executivesummary: A standalone, zero-dependency Go CLI tool to rapidly generate boilerplate code for internal microservices.
plan.architectureoverview: A local command-line application built with Go and the Cobra framework, executing locally to generate files based on embedded templates.
plan.technologystack: Go, Cobra (CLI framework), Go templates, goreleaser.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: 1. Command parsing 2. Template execution 3. File system writing 4. Git initialization.
plan.systemcomponents: CLI Binary, Embedded Template Files, Local File System interface.
plan.workflows: Developer runs `scaffold init` -> CLI prompts for service name -> Renders Go templates -> Writes to disk -> Runs `git init`.
plan.datamodel: No persistent data model. Uses in-memory structs to bind user input to template variables.
plan.apidesign: Command-line interface only. No network APIs exposed.
plan.securityandcompliance: All released binaries are cryptographically signed using cosign during the CI build process. Checksums (SHA256) are generated and published alongside releases.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Distribute the Go CLI as signed binaries for macOS, Linux, and Windows through GitHub Releases. Use goreleaser for CI packaging, checksum generation, and semantic-versioned release promotion. An internal Homebrew tap is automatically updated with the new formulae and checksums.
plan.observability: Opt-in anonymous telemetry sent to an internal collector endpoint. Logs are written to stdout/stderr locally.
plan.costandscaling: Negligible cost. Scaling handled naturally as execution is entirely distributed on client machines.
plan.phasedimplementation: v1.0 supports Go service templates only.
plan.developmentguidelines: Local testing uses `go run`. New templates must include unit tests verifying generated output compiles.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Embedding templates increases binary size slightly, but removes external dependencies, aligning with constraints.
plan.openquestionsresolved: Binary signing strategy finalized using goreleaser.

target_output.thinkingsummary: This is a revision round. The architecture perfectly matches the simple CLI profile, provides concrete operational details, and is internally consistent. I will issue high rubric scores and close the issue.
target_output.summary: The architecture meaningfully improved by fully resolving the binary signature requirement. The plan is highly cohesive and ready for implementation.
strengths: Excellent use of goreleaser for automated, secure distribution.
Perfect alignment with the zero-dependency constraint by utilizing embedded templates.
Clear, implementation-grade operational details for a CLI tool.
recommendations: Proceed with implementation as designed.

target_output.thinkingsummary: First pass audit. The plan explicitly substitutes the mandated local Llama-3 deployment with the public OpenAI GPT-4 API. Given the 'confidential' legal data, 'high' risk, and strict 'air-gap' constraints, sending attorney-client privileged documents to a third-party API is a critical security and compliance violation. This drops security and alignment scores drastically.
target_output.summary: The architecture introduces a catastrophic compliance and security breach by explicitly planning to send highly confidential legal documents to a public third-party API, violating the core local-deployment constraint.
strengths: Clear API design and standard microservice architecture.
Good use of HashiCorp Vault for secret management.
concerns: Using OpenAI GPT-4 API directly violates the zero data sharing and local LLM mandates.
Sending legal discovery documents to a public internet service breaches attorney-client privilege constraints.
The plan explicitly overrides confirmed legal requirements for perceived 'quality' improvements.
blockingissues: Use of public OpenAI API violates strict data sovereignty and local model execution constraints for confidential legal data.
recommendations: Immediately replace the OpenAI integration with a local vLLM deployment running Llama-3.
Ensure no network egress is permitted from the LLM processing pods to the public internet.

target_output.thinkingsummary: Revision round 3. The author attempted to fix the local encryption issue (SEC-030) by adding biometric app unlock. However, app-level biometric locks do not encrypt the underlying SQLite database file. The frozen contract explicitly requires SQLCipher. Storing unencrypted PHI on a mobile device remains a critical HIPAA violation. The issue is unchanged.
target_output.summary: The plan regressed or stayed roughly flat. While biometric unlock was added, the critical requirement to encrypt the local database (SQLCipher) for HIPAA compliance remains ignored.
strengths: Good implementation of offline sync patterns.
Solid CI/CD pipeline definition using Fastlane.
concerns: Biometric app lock does not encrypt the SQLite file at rest.
Unencrypted PHI on mobile devices is a critical regulatory violation (HIPAA).
blockingissues: Local SQLite database storing PHI remains unencrypted.
recommendations: Replace `react-native-sqlite-storage` with a SQLCipher-compatible library.
Ensure the database encryption key is securely derived and stored in the device's secure enclave/keystore.

projectgoal.value: Open-source Typescript library for precise financial arithmetic
projectgoal.rationale: Standardize rounding and decimal math across company projects

featurescope.value: Arbitrary precision arithmetic, standard financial rounding modes

securitybaseline.value: Zero dependencies, strict linting, SAST scanning in CI

constraints.value: Must be universally compatible (Node.js, Browser, Edge runtimes)

devops.publish: Automate NPM publish via GitHub Actions.

reasonerreviews.architectreasoner: Clean, perfect plan. Hits all constraints, specifies the build tools perfectly.

specialistsubplans.backend: Using BigInt internally for arbitrary precision where applicable, wrapped in a clean API.

plan.thinkingsummary: Designing a zero-dependency, highly tested TypeScript library for financial math. Ensuring dual-build outputs (ESM/CJS) and strict CI pipelines to guarantee 100% test coverage and no supply chain vulnerabilities.
plan.title: FinMath.js SDK
plan.executivesummary: A robust, zero-dependency TypeScript library providing arbitrary-precision arithmetic and financial rounding utilities for Node, Edge, and Browser environments.
plan.architectureoverview: The project is structured as a collection of pure functions. It utilizes TypeScript for source code, compiled down via tsup to both CommonJS and ECMAScript Modules.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: 1. BigInt Wrapper 2. Decimal Arithmetic 3. Rounding Modes (Half-Even, Floor, Ceil).
plan.systemcomponents: Source Code (src/), Test Suite (test/), CI Pipeline (.github/workflows).
plan.workflows: Developer pushes code -> CI runs Vitest, ESLint, and CodeQL -> Merges to main -> Semantic Release tags and publishes to NPM.
plan.datamodel: Immutable state. All operations return new instances of the `Currency` object.
plan.securityandcompliance: Strictly zero runtime dependencies to eliminate supply chain attack surface. CI pipeline includes GitHub Advanced Security (CodeQL) and npm audit.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Automated deployment to the public NPM registry using `semantic-release`. Provenance assertions enabled for NPM publishes.
plan.observability: Vitest configured to fail the CI build if statement, branch, or function coverage drops below 100%.
plan.costandscaling: No runtime hosting costs. Executes entirely within the consuming application's runtime.
plan.phasedimplementation: MVP covers core arithmetic. V2 will introduce complex financial formulas.
plan.developmentguidelines: All functions must be pure. No mutations allowed. Floating-point math (`+`, `-`) is strictly forbidden internally.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Using strings and BigInt for precision is slightly slower than native floats, but accuracy is paramount for financial systems.
plan.openquestionsresolved: Confirmed `tsup` will be used to handle the dual CJS/ESM builds easily.

target_output.thinkingsummary: First pass audit. This is an exceptionally clean and well-thought-out plan for a library/SDK. It flawlessly adheres to the zero-dependency constraint, the dual-build execution preference, and the 100% test coverage observability baseline. Security is well-handled via CI/CD provenance and SAST. Rubric scores will be near-perfect.
target_output.summary: An excellent, highly detailed, and fully compliant architecture plan. All constraints and requirements are met with concrete, appropriate technology choices.
strengths: Perfect alignment with the zero-dependency security baseline.
Concrete, modern build tooling choices (tsup, Vitest, semantic-release).
Clear understanding of the library SDK execution context (Edge, Node, Browser).
recommendations: Proceed directly to implementation.

plan.title: NextGen Retail POS
plan.executivesummary: An Electron-based Point of Sale desktop application for retail stores, supporting hardware peripherals and robust offline cash processing.
plan.architectureoverview: React frontend runs in the Electron renderer. Node.js main process handles serial connections to receipt printers and barcode scanners, and connects to the payment gateway.
plan.functionalfeaturemap: 1. Catalog Browsing 2. Hardware I/O 3. Payment Processing 4. Offline Queue.
plan.systemcomponents: Electron App, Peripheral Drivers (Serial/USB), Payment Terminal Integration.
plan.workflows: Cashier scans item -> UI updates -> Selects 'Cash' -> If offline, saves to LevelDB -> Background syncs to cloud when online.
plan.datamodel: LevelDB stores product catalog (synced daily) and pending offline transactions.
plan.apidesign: Renderer communicates with Main process via IPC bridges. Main process calls Cloud REST APIs.
plan.securityandcompliance: Credit card data is captured via the payment terminal and passed through the main process to the gateway. Card numbers are not saved to LevelDB.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Packaged as an MSI using electron-builder and deployed via Microsoft InTune to all store terminals.
plan.observability: Integrated Sentry for crash reporting. Sentry is configured to upload full local unmasked crash dumps and memory states from the Electron process to aid debugging.
plan.phasedimplementation: Pilot in 5 stores, followed by national rollout.
plan.developmentguidelines: IPC channels must be strictly typed.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Electron apps consume high memory, POS hardware must have at least 8GB RAM.
plan.openquestionsresolved: Offline cash flow designed and approved.

target_output.thinkingsummary: Revision round 2. The author successfully fixed the offline cash transaction issue (ARC-044). However, in the process of adding observability, they configured Sentry to capture 'full local unmasked crash dumps and memory states'. Since this process handles raw card data, sending unmasked memory dumps to a cloud logging provider is a severe PCI-DSS violation and directly conflicts with the security baseline requiring logs to be scrubbed of PCI data. This introduces a new critical issue.
target_output.summary: While the offline cash functionality was successfully implemented, the architecture regressed severely in security. Introducing unmasked memory crash dumps into a PCI-scoped application introduces a critical compliance violation.
strengths: Good resolution of the offline cash constraint using LevelDB.
Solid Electron IPC architecture for hardware peripheral isolation.
concerns: Unmasked crash dumps from memory will inevitably capture raw Primary Account Numbers (PANs).
Uploading PCI data to a standard Sentry instance violates PCI-DSS Level 1 and the explicit observability baseline.
blockingissues: Sentry configuration uploads unmasked memory crash dumps, risking exposure of raw PANs.
recommendations: Immediately disable full memory crash dumps in Sentry.
Implement rigorous scrubbing middleware before any error data leaves the terminal.

requirementconflicts.currentvalue: Upload full local unmasked crash dumps and memory states
requirementconflicts.proposedvalue: Centralized error reporting, scrubbed of all PII/PCI data
requirementconflicts.exactreason: The plan explicitly uploads unmasked memory dumps, violating the requirement to scrub PCI data.

target_output.thinkingsummary: First pass audit. The plan explicitly states that responsiveness relies on `window.innerWidth` checks directly in main component bodies. This completely breaks Server-Side Rendering (SSR) in React/Next.js because the `window` object is undefined on the server. This is a critical failure of the explicit SSR constraint.
target_output.summary: The proposed architecture directly violates the SSR constraint by utilizing browser-only globals (`window`) in the component body scope.
strengths: Good alignment with packaging and deployment requirements.
concerns: Direct use of `window` outside of `useEffect` breaks SSR.
blockingissues: Implementation relies on browser-only APIs in the main render path, breaking required SSR compatibility.
recommendations: Refactor responsive hooks to use CSS media queries (via Tailwind) where possible.
If JS-based sizing is necessary, ensure `window` is only accessed within `useEffect` hooks or after a mounted check.

Relying solely on 'system prompts' for PII protection is a severe security failure for a public-facing bot. This is a critical security violation.

recommendations: Implement a streaming redaction proxy (e.g., using Presidio with chunked processing) that inspects tokens before forwarding them to the client.

concerns: Piping HTTP curl directly to shell

reasonerreviews.security: Check Electron isolation

plan.thinkingsummary: Building Electron video editor. Setting nodeIntegration to true so the React frontend can directly call FFMPEG binaries without dealing with IPC overhead.
plan.title: Video Editor Pro
plan.executivesummary: Electron desktop app for video editing.
plan.architectureoverview: Electron shell running React frontend manipulating video via FFMPEG.
plan.workflows: User clicks cut, React calls Node `child_process.exec` directly to run FFMPEG.
plan.securityandcompliance: `nodeIntegration` is set to `true` and `contextIsolation` is `false` to allow the frontend to easily spawn child processes for FFMPEG.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Electron Builder.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Disabling contextIsolation and enabling nodeIntegration allows any XSS vulnerability in the React UI to execute arbitrary commands on the user's OS.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The plan explicitly enables `nodeIntegration` and disables `contextIsolation`. This is a critical Electron security flaw allowing XSS to escalate to Remote Code Execution (RCE), directly violating the 'Secure IPC' execution preference and the explicit 'Renderer must be completely isolated' requirement.
target_output.summary: Critical security vulnerability. Electron configured with dangerous privileges.
strengths: FFMPEG appropriately chosen for video processing
blockingissues: Electron security configuration allows RCE
recommendations: Enable contextIsolation, disable nodeIntegration, and use a secure ContextBridge for all FFMPEG invocations

requirementconflicts.exactreason: Disabling context isolation violates the requirement for secure IPC communication in Electron.

issueupdates.title: Insecure Electron Config
issueupdates.detail: Newly introduced. Evidence: securityandcompliance explicitly states `nodeIntegration` is set to `true` and `contextIsolation` is `false`. Impact: lowers security and requirementsalignment critically.

plan.title: Video Editor Pro v2
plan.executivesummary: Secure Electron desktop app.
plan.architectureoverview: Electron shell running React with secure IPC bridge.
plan.workflows: User clicks cut, React calls `window.api.processVideo()` over IPC. Node main process reads the entire video file into a `Buffer` using `fs.readFileSync`, processes it, and writes it back out.
plan.securityandcompliance: `nodeIntegration` is `false` and `contextIsolation` is `true`. Standard OS permissions.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Electron Builder.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Reading large video files into memory via `fs.readFileSync` will cause the Node process to crash with Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors on files larger than ~2GB.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The IPC security issue is resolved. However, reading entire video files into a Node buffer using `fs.readFileSync` is an egregious architectural error for a video editor. This will cause catastrophic OOM crashes, violating operability standards for media processing.
target_output.summary: IPC secured, but introduced a severe operability and architecture flaw in file handling.
blockingissues: Cannot load large media files entirely into memory
recommendations: Use Node.js `fs.createReadStream` or pass file paths directly to FFMPEG rather than loading data into Node buffers

issueupdates.title: Insecure Electron Config

target_output.thinkingsummary: The plan explicitly enables `nodeIntegration` and disables `contextIsolation`. This is a critical Electron security flaw allowing XSS to escalate to Remote Code Execution (RCE), directly violating the 'Secure IPC' execution preference and the explicit 'Renderer must be completely isolated' requirement.
target_output.summary: Critical security vulnerability. Electron configured with dangerous privileges.
strengths: Uses local JSON and execution properly
blockingissues: Electron security configuration allows RCE
recommendations: Enable contextIsolation, disable nodeIntegration, and use a secure ContextBridge for all child process invocations

requirementconflicts.exactreason: Disabling context isolation violates the requirement for secure IPC communication in Electron.

plan.title: Game Server Manager v2
plan.executivesummary: Secure Electron desktop app.
plan.architectureoverview: Electron shell running React with secure IPC bridge.
plan.workflows: App boots. Node main process reads the entire game asset file into a buffer using `fs.readFileSync` to calculate SHA256, then starts the server. User clicks start, React calls `window.api.startServer()` over IPC.
plan.securityandcompliance: `nodeIntegration` is `false` and `contextIsolation` is `true`. Standard OS permissions.
plan.deploymentandoperations: Electron Builder.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: Reading massive 10GB game files into memory via `fs.readFileSync` will cause the Node process to crash with Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The IPC security issue is resolved. However, reading entire 10GB game asset files into a Node buffer using `fs.readFileSync` is a terrible architectural error. This will cause catastrophic OOM crashes, completely breaking operability.
target_output.summary: IPC secured, but introduced a severe operability and architecture flaw in file handling.
blockingissues: Cannot load large data files entirely into memory
recommendations: Use Node.js `crypto.createHash` combined with `fs.createReadStream` to calculate hashes efficiently

plan.thinkingsummary: Built a highly secure, implementation-grade plan addressing all requirements including VPC peering and OWASP mitigations.
plan.title: Wealth Management Dashboard
plan.executivesummary: Secure frontend for wealth clients.
plan.architectureoverview: React SPA interacting with Java Spring microservices deployed on AWS ECS. Core banking data fetched via VPC Peering.
plan.systemcomponents: ALB, WAF, ECS Fargate, RDS, Core Banking DB.
plan.workflows: User logs in via Auth0 (MFA) -> Spring API queries local RDS for preferences and core banking via VPC Peering for balances -> React displays data.
plan.datamodel: Relational profile data in RDS. Core financial data remains in core banking system.
plan.securityandcompliance: AWS WAF on ALB to mitigate OWASP Top 10 (SQLi/XSS). Spring Security handles CSRF/CORS. DB encryption at rest. Traffic to core banking strictly routed over encrypted VPC Peering.
plan.deploymentandoperations: GitLab CI deploys via Terraform.
plan.risksandtradeoffs: VPC peering latency.

target_output.thinkingsummary: The architecture fully aligns with the contract, properly outlining the VPC peering constraint, OWASP mitigations via AWS WAF, and a clear, implementation-grade workflow.
target_output.summary: Excellent, highly detailed plan that securely addresses all functional and non-functional constraints for a high-risk financial application.
strengths: Clear AWS WAF and Spring Security integration for OWASP mitigations.
Correctly implements VPC peering to the core banking system.
